coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots from bitter gourd for effective metal sensing and biological applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Renuga, V.
Vinoliya, X.
Dinesh, A.
Suthakaran, S.
Ayyar, Manikandan
Mohanavel, V.
Gnanasekaran, Lalitha
Paramasivam, Prabhu
Bousbih, R.
Hossain, M. Khalid
Rajendran, A.
Santhoshkumar, S.
coredata.dc:description: Highly fluorescent Carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized from the Bitter Gourd by simple hydrothermal method without the addition of any surfactant or additives. The optical properties, luminescence, phase formation and crystal structure were measured using UV–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Photoluminescence (PL), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) by varying the medium from acidic to basic by changing its pH (2−12) and temperature range (180-300οC) to assess the exact nature and properties of the synthesized C-dots. The synthesized C-dots have wide range of applications from metal sensing to bioimaging and optoelectronics. The antimicrobial properties of the prepared C-dots were also analyzed using Klebsiellapneumoniae and Staphylococcus aureus microbes and found that the prepared C-dots have better antimicrobial activity in both microbes with better efficiency due to its smaller size. The challenges and perspectives of the synthesized C-dots from Bitter Gourd were also discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hydrothermal method
C-dots
XRD
TEM
Metal sensing
Biological activities

Antibacterial analysis of the synthesized C-dots
Cytotoxicity study of the synthesized C-dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra of effect of (a) acidic and (b) basic pH and (c) temperature on C-dots.
UV–Vis spectra of effect of (a) acidic and (b) basic and (c) temperature on C-dots.
PL Spectra of effect of (a) acidic (b) basic pH and (c) effect of temperature on C-dots.
(a) X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern and (b) TEM images of C-dots.
Photostability analysis of the as-fabricated C-dots.
(a) PL of the metal sensing C-dots and (b) Metal ions response towards C-dots.
(a,b) Antibacterial analysis and (c,d) zone of inhibition of C-dots using (a) Klebsiella pneumonia and (b) Staphylococcus aureus organism.
Cytotoxicity Study of the synthesized C-dots.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of biocompatible fluorescent carbon dots from bitter gourd for effective metal sensing and biological applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Highly fluorescent Carbon dots (C-dots) were synthesized from the Bitter Gourd by simple hydrothermal method without the addition of any surfactant or additives. The optical properties, luminescence, phase formation and crystal structure were measured using UV–Visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis), Photoluminescence (PL), X-ray diffraction (XRD) and Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) by varying the medium from acidic to basic by changing its pH (2−12) and temperature range (180-300οC) to assess the exact nature and properties of the synthesized C-dots. The synthesized C-dots have wide range of applications from metal sensing to bioimaging and optoelectronics. The antimicrobial properties of the prepared C-dots were also analyzed using Klebsiellapneumoniae and Staphylococcus aureus microbes and found that the prepared C-dots have better antimicrobial activity in both microbes with better efficiency due to its smaller size. The challenges and perspectives of the synthesized C-dots from Bitter Gourd were also discussed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) play a vital role in most of the biological and sensing purposes, but it has some limitations due to its toxic nature [1–5]. To overcome such limitations Carbon dots (C-dots) fulfill all the properties having excellent optical, photo stability and emission spectra with large stokes shifts but with good biocompatibilities and low toxic nature [6–10]. The higher hydrophilic and easily modifiable nature of C-dots, enable it to be a better biocompatible candidate in most of the bioimaging, biochemical analysis and photo-electrocatalysis [11–15]. The present work studies the effect of pH and temperature over the prepared C-dots (Bitter Gourd) by hydrothermal method [16–20]. The properties of prepared C-dots, such as luminescence, optical and phase structure were studied in both acidic and basic medium by varying pH from 2 to 12. At the same time, the effects of temperature (180–300 °C) were also analyzed. In order to find out any medium or temperature that may have any influence on the properties of the synthesized C-dots [21–24]. The synthesized C-dots have excellent sensing as well as biological properties, and it is confirmed by studying their metal sensing, bioimaging and antimicrobial studies.

The present study involves synthesizing of C-dots from Bitter Gourd by hydrothermal method [25]. The fresh Bitter Gourd was initially cleaned according to established protocols and then placed in a ceramic crucible. It was carbonized at temperatures of 180, 220, 260, and 300 °C for a duration of 5 h in a tube furnace, with a heating rate of 10 °C per minute. After cooling 50 mL of distilled water was added to the obtained black product, and made to disperse through ultra-sonication for 30 min. The black solution containing C-dots and larger carbon particles was subjected to filtration using a 0.22 μm membrane. Subsequently, the filtrate was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 30 min to eliminate the larger particles. The solution containing the supernatant C-dots was maintained at 4 °C for additional characterization.

The prepared C-dots' structural information on the basis of its acidic (2 to 6 pH) as well as basic (pH 8 to 12) influences and their effect on temperature from 180 to 360 °C were recorded and were shown in Fig. 1 (a-c) and confirmed the presence of carbonyl compound, H2O and some aromatic phenolic groups. The incorporation of these groups on carbon dots' surfaces results in superior water solubility and stability [26–30]. For both acidic and basic pH there were not any appreciable changes in the carbonyl (1630 cm−1) and OH (3421–3416 cm−1) stretching frequencies. The carbonyl stretching frequency (1602 cm−1) for C-dots varies slightly at weaker acidic condition. Similarly, the CH stretching group frequency for aromatic ring (678 cm−1) also maintains the same range for both acidic and basic medium.

The temperature ranges used for the present study are 180, 220, 260 and 300 °C·In all cases the stretching frequency for OH occurs from 3457 to 3442 cm−1 and the corresponding carbonyl frequency occurs at 1637 cm−1. The aromatic CH stretching frequency of phenolic group appears from 672 to 667 cm−1 in all cases. Since, carbonization starts after 150–170 °C, the present work concentrates from 180 to 300 °C to find the effect of temperature. The above result concludes that either pH or temperature will not affect the Bitter Gourd's composition.

Fig. 2 (a-c) displays the UV–Visible spectra recorded for the synthesized C-dots. The optical absorption peak for these C-dots appears within the range of 277 to 288 nm at acidic pH, whereas it is noted between 253 and 263 nm at basic pH. Temperature variations (180–280 °C) will also exhibit absorption in the range of 278 nm. The existence of absorption peak in the above range is mainly due to π-π⁎ transition of CC, is the characteristic peak of the C-dot prepared by the carbonization of carbon-based materials and suggest that the possibility of only one surface state present on the carbon dots surface [30]. This reiterates the identification of a carbonyl group in the C-dot, confirmed through the n-π* transition and the π-π* transition of the conjugated CO and CC bonds. The data suggests that the synthesized carbon dots maintain stability across all tested pH and temperature ranges. This can be ascribed to their small size and the similar electrostatic repulsions created by the negatively charged functional groups on their surfaces.

The PL spectra of the synthesized C-dots at various acidic and basic pH values along with different temperature range (180–300 °C) were recorded and shown in Fig. 3 (a-c). In all cases, both acidic and basic PL emission exhibits blue emission range from 450 to 485 nm. The photoluminescent properties of carbon dots in this scenario are determined by two main factors, the presence of particles of varying sizes and the distribution of different surface energy traps on their surfaces [31–33].

The XRD pattern of the synthesized C-dot from Bitter Gourd is shown in Fig. 4(a). The appearance of broad diffraction around 2θ = 25° attributes to (002) plane indicating the existence of amorphous nature of C-dots [34]. The detection of the (002) plane in the XRD spectra of the produced C-dots substantiates the amorphous nature and elevated oxygen levels, in the form of water, present in bitter gourd. Additionally, the weak peaks observed at 2θ = 44° and 52°, corresponding to the (101) and (004) planes, indicate the presence of carbon in the form of carbonyl groups and various phenolic functional groups on the surface of carbon dots. Since these functional groups are present in a random manner their appearance in the spectra depicted as more of noisy structure [35]. The phase structure of the synthesized C-dot is hexagonal (JCPDF file: 89–8487) and matched very well with many of the literature values. The cell parameters ‘a’ and ‘c’ are found from the graph and shown as a = 2.440 Å and c = 7.039 Å, respectively.

To investigate the morphology of the synthesized carbon dots, a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was performed. The TEM image clearly indicates that the synthesized C-dots are spherical, with diameters falling within the range of 5 to 9 nm, as presented in Fig. 4(b).

The photostability of the as-fabricated C-dots was also investigated by measuring the PL intensity of the C-dot after storage for 6 months in the dark at 4 °C (Fig. 5 ). The fluorescence as quantified at given time intervals using spectrofluorometer showed that the C-dots maintained 90–95 % of their initial intensity and did not shift the emission peak further, thus suggesting that the synthesized C-dots had affixed photostability and was rather reluctant to aggregation and structural alteration. This stability is due to a durable carbon core as well as an efficient surface functionalization, which does not allow for oxidation or photobleaching. These results bear evidence about the applicability of C-dots for long term use in various areas such as bioimaging, sensing and optoelectronics [36,37]. Most of the missing data/analysis related to the present study has been incorporated in the revised manuscript.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications of the synthesized C-dots from bitter gourd
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized C-dots exhibit excellent metal sensing and biological applications. Through PL measurements by analyzing their quenching rates the metal ions present in the particular systems can be detected. For biological applications, the existence of antimicrobial and cytotoxicity analysis was made through disc diffusion method and MTT assay method. Their analysis reports were furnished in the following sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion sensing capability of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized C-dots are highly suitable for sensing applications due to the presence of oxygen-containing groups on their surface, which can effectively coordinate with transition metal ions and demonstrate exceptional fluorescence properties. The current study investigating the potential of synthesized C-dots as a fluorescent probe for the quantitative analysis of metal ions. The evaluation of the sensing potential of the produced C-dots involved monitoring their fluorescence intensity when exposed to different metal ions, encompassing alkali, alkaline earth, and transition metals. The corresponding alterations in the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots due to the presence of these metal ions are presented in Fig. 6 .

The metal ions used in the present study are K+, Li+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, and Zn2+ from their corresponding metal chlorides. Out of the 10 kinds of metal ions, transition metal ions only explored better quenching ability than the other alkali and alkaline earth metals. Even among the transition metal ions Fe3+ quenches very effectively compared to remaining metals. The reason behind in this aspect is that Fe3+ can effectively coordinate with hydroxyl group present on the surface of C-dots. Hence it can form strong Fe(H2O)6 complex, so that it can quench effectively compared to other metal ions [38–40]. As compared to alkali metals, alkaline earth metals like Ca2+ and Ba2+ exhibit higher intensity than pure C-dots. The observed peak intensity in this instance may result from the suppression of non-radiative recombination between electrons from the metals and holes present on the surface of the C-dots. Therefore, correspondingly their intensity increases reasonably. The findings indicate that the C-dots derived from Bitter Gourd serve as an effective fluorescence probe for Fe3+ ions, demonstrating potential applications across various sectors, including industrial and biological fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial analysis of the synthesized C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized C-dots from Bitter Gourd were tested for antibacterial properties against Gram-negative and Gram-positive by performing disk diffusion assay and their corresponding results are shown in Fig. 7 (a-b). The two bacteria used in the present study are Klebsiella pneumoniae and Staphylococcus aureus. The antibacterial properties of the C-dots synthesized from Bitter Gourd with different concentrations exhibit a positive correlation between the area of zone of inhibition and the concentration of the C-dots used were analyzed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity study of the synthesized C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Effective bioimaging necessitates that the fluorescent marker chosen has both advantageous optical characteristics and low levels of cytotoxicity. The cytotoxic effects of C-Dots were determined by assessing the viability of A549 lung cancer cells treated with C-Dots, utilizing the MTT assay for measurement. The A549 lung cancer cell line was incubated with a range of C-dot doses for 24 and 48 h, respectively. The cell viability of C-dots against lung cancer cell (A549) is shown in Fig. 8 . Thus, the viability of the cells remained above 95 % even after being incubated for 24 h with a concentration of 100 μg/ml of C-Dots, which demonstrates that the C-Dots had very low toxicity towards the cells.

The procedure typically followed for the MTT assay in this research is described below. C-dot concentrations tested ranged from 1 to 100 μg, and their cytotoxicity was assessed in the A549 cancer cell line using the MTT assay. The experiments were carried out under standard conditions of 5 % CO2 and 37 °C, with evaluations of cell viability conducted at 24 and 48-h time points. The findings demonstrate that the synthesized carbon dots derived from Bitter Gourd show minimal cytotoxicity, maintaining approximately 95.5 % cell viability at a concentration of 250 μg/mL after 24 h and at 100 μg/mL after 48 h. The MTT assay results indicated that the C-dots synthesized from Bitter Gourd exhibit low toxicity, remaining non-toxic at concentrations up to 100 μg/mL over a 24-h period, with a cell viability of approximately 95.5 %. This suggests minimal cytotoxic effects and good biocompatibility of the C-dots, making them suitable for future applications in in vitro cell imaging.

In summary, the present work investigated the synthesis of C-dots from Bitter Gourd by greener method using simple hydrothermal method. The FTIR and UV–Vis spectral analysis were measured at different acidic, basic conditions and with temperature variations from 180 to 300 °C. FT-IR results were confirmed that there was no composition change on either varying pH or temperature range on C-dots. The UV–Vis spectral measurements revealed that the presence of carbonyl and CC group of phenolic groups on the surface of C-dots exhibit blue emission. The PL measurement revealed that on both acidic and basic conditions, the PL intensity increased in acidic medium whereas, decreased in basic medium due to the random distribution of the particles on the surface of C-dots. XRD analysis of the prepared C-dots revealed the existence of hexagonal phase structure. TEM analysis also revealed that the existence of spherical shape with size range of 30 nm. The synthesized C-dots act as a best Fe3+ ion sensor and can be used in many industries and biological fields. The antimicrobial analysis revealed that the synthesized C-dots shows higher antibacterial activity with increasing concentrations of C-dots. The very low toxic nature of the synthesized C-dots from Bitter Gourd was confirmed by their cytotoxicity analysis.

V. Renuga: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. X. Vinoliya: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. A. Dinesh: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. S. Suthakaran: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation. Manikandan Ayyar: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. V. Mohanavel: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. Lalitha Gnanasekaran: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. Prabhu Paramasivam: Resources, Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. R. Bousbih: Validation, Visualization, Writing – review & editing. M. Khalid Hossain: Investigation, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. A. Rajendran: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation. S. Santhoshkumar: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
